steps_template: Prepare all ingredients: wash, cut, and measure. Keep within arm's reach.
Heat wok or pan over high heat until smoking. Add oil and swirl to coat.
Add aromatics (garlic, ginger, onion) and stir for 10-15 seconds until fragrant.
Add protein and stir-fry until just cooked through, about 2-3 minutes. Remove and set aside.
Add harder vegetables first (carrots, broccoli stems) and stir-fry 1-2 minutes.
Add softer vegetables (bell peppers, snow peas) and stir-fry 1 minute.
Return protein to pan. Add sauce and toss everything together for 30 seconds.
Serve immediately over rice or noodles.
tips: Keep ingredients moving in the pan. Cut everything to similar size for even cooking.

steps_template: Heat wok over highest heat until smoking.
Add oil, then immediately add protein or main ingredient.
Stir constantly for 1-2 minutes.
Add seasonings and sauce.
Toss for 30 seconds and serve immediately.
tips: Everything must be prepped before turning on the heat. The whole cook takes under 3 minutes.

steps_template: Preheat oven to the required temperature.
Prepare ingredients: season protein, chop vegetables, and arrange on a baking sheet or in a dish.
Drizzle with oil and season with salt, pepper, and desired spices.
Place in the center rack of the preheated oven.
Bake for the specified time, flipping or stirring halfway through if needed.
Check doneness with a thermometer or by texture. Rest for 3-5 minutes before serving.
tips: Use a light-colored baking sheet for more even browning. Line with parchment for easy cleanup.

steps_template: Preheat air fryer to the required temperature.
Pat ingredients dry with paper towels for maximum crispiness.
Lightly coat with oil using a spray or brush.
Arrange in a single layer in the basket. Do not overcrowd.
Air fry for the specified time, shaking or flipping halfway through.
Check for desired crispiness. Let rest 2 minutes before serving.
tips: Shake the basket halfway for even crisping. A light oil spray gives better results than drizzling.

steps_template: Fill a pot with water or broth. Add a pinch of salt.
Bring to a rolling boil over high heat.
Add ingredients in order of cooking time: longest first, shortest last.
Reduce heat to maintain a gentle simmer (small bubbles breaking the surface).
Simmer for the specified time until ingredients are tender.
Taste and adjust seasoning. Serve hot.
tips: Salt the water generously. Add delicate herbs at the very end.

steps_template: Fill a pot with 1-2 inches of water. Bring to a boil.
Place steamer basket above the water level. Ensure water does not touch the basket.
Arrange ingredients in the steamer basket in a single layer.
Cover tightly and steam over medium heat for the specified time.
Check doneness by piercing with a fork or chopstick.
Remove carefully (watch for steam). Season and serve immediately.
tips: Line the basket with cabbage leaves or parchment to prevent sticking. Keep the lid closed.

steps_template: Pat protein completely dry with paper towels. Season generously.
Heat a heavy-bottomed pan over medium-high heat until very hot.
Add a thin layer of high-smoke-point oil.
Place protein away from you into the pan. Do not move it.
Sear undisturbed for 3-4 minutes until a golden crust forms.
Flip once and sear the other side. Add butter, garlic, and herbs in the last minute for basting.
Rest for 3-5 minutes before slicing.
tips: A dry surface is the key to a good sear. If the pan isn't hot enough, the protein will steam instead.

steps_template: Fill a deep pot with oil to at least 3 inches deep. Heat to the required temperature.
Pat ingredients dry and coat with batter or breading if desired.
Carefully lower ingredients into the hot oil using a slotted spoon or spider.
Fry in small batches to maintain oil temperature.
Turn occasionally for even browning. Fry until golden and cooked through.
Remove with a slotted spoon and drain on paper towels. Season immediately.
tips: Use a thermometer to maintain oil temperature. Too low = greasy. Too high = burnt outside, raw inside.

steps_template: Season and sear protein in a hot pot until browned on all sides. Remove and set aside.
Sauté aromatics (onion, garlic, ginger, scallions) in the same pot until softened.
Add aromatics and spices (star anise, cinnamon, bay leaves) and toast for 30 seconds.
Deglaze with wine, soy sauce, or broth, scraping up browned bits.
Return protein to pot. Add enough liquid to come halfway up the protein.
Bring to a boil, then reduce to a very low simmer. Cover with a tight lid.
Cook for 1-3 hours (or until fork-tender), checking occasionally.
Adjust seasoning. Serve with the braising liquid as a sauce.
tips: Low and slow is the rule. A tight lid is essential. The liquid should barely bubble.

steps_template: Brown protein in a hot pot. Remove and set aside.
Sauté aromatics in the same pot.
Return protein. Add liquid (broth, water, wine) to cover.
Bring to a boil, then reduce to lowest heat. Cover.
Cook for 2-4 hours until very tender.
Adjust seasoning. Serve hot.
tips: Resist the urge to raise the heat. Check liquid level occasionally.

steps_template: Bring a large pot of heavily salted water to a rolling boil.
Prepare an ice bath in a large bowl nearby.
Add vegetables to the boiling water. Cook for 1-3 minutes until bright green and crisp-tender.
Immediately transfer to the ice bath using a slotted spoon or strainer.
Let cool completely in the ice bath (about 2 minutes).
Drain well and pat dry. Use immediately or store.
tips: Don't overcrowd the pot. Ice bath stops cooking instantly.

steps_template: Rinse rice in cold water until water runs clear (3-4 rinses).
Drain rice and add to the rice cooker bowl.
Add water in the correct ratio (1:1.2 for white rice, 1:1.5 for brown rice).
Optionally add a pinch of salt and a tiny drop of oil.
Close the lid and press the cook button.
When the cycle finishes, let it steam with the lid closed for 10 minutes.
Fluff with a rice paddle and serve.
tips: Rinsing removes excess starch for fluffier rice. Letting it steam after cooking is essential.

steps_template: Wash and prepare all fresh ingredients.
Cook any grains or proteins separately if needed.
Chop vegetables to desired size and shape.
Make the dressing or sauce by whisking together the ingredients.
Combine all components in a large bowl.
Toss gently with dressing just before serving.
Season with salt, pepper, and fresh herbs to taste.
tips: Keep dressing separate until serving to prevent sogginess.

steps_template: Blanch meat bones in boiling water for 5 minutes. Drain and rinse.
Place blanched bones in a clean pot with fresh cold water.
Add aromatics (ginger, scallion, goji dates) and bring to a boil.
Reduce to a gentle simmer. Skim any foam that rises to the surface.
Simmer for 1-3 hours for bone broth, 30-60 minutes for vegetable soup.
Add vegetables in the last 15-20 minutes.
Season with salt just before serving.
tips: Start with cold water for clearer broth. Never boil a soup you want clear.

steps_template: Blanch vegetables or protein in boiling water until just cooked.
Shock in ice water to stop cooking and keep crisp.
Drain thoroughly and cut into bite-sized pieces.
Prepare the dressing: mix soy sauce, vinegar, sesame oil, garlic, and chili oil.
Pour dressing over ingredients and toss well.
Garnish with sesame seeds, scallions, or cilantro.
Let sit for 5-10 minutes for flavors to meld before serving.
tips: The key is the dressing ratio. Taste and adjust before tossing.

steps_template: Soak clay pot in water for 15 minutes before first use.
Heat pot over medium heat with oil.
Add aromatics (ginger, garlic) and cook until fragrant.
Add main ingredients and pour in sauce/broth.
Bring to a boil, then reduce to low heat.
Cover and simmer for 15-25 minutes.
Serve directly in the clay pot — it retains heat beautifully.
tips: Never put a cold clay pot on high heat — it will crack. Always start with medium.

steps_template: Prepare the broth: spicy (mala) or mild (bone broth) in a divided pot.
Bring broth to a boil at the table.
Prepare thinly sliced proteins, vegetables, tofu, and noodles on separate plates.
Each person cooks their own ingredients by dipping into the boiling broth.
Cook proteins for 10-30 seconds (thin slices) or 2-3 minutes (thicker pieces).
Dip cooked items in personal sauce (sesame paste, garlic soy, etc.).
Add noodles or rice to the broth at the end for a flavorful finish.
tips: Thinly sliced meat cooks in seconds. Don't overload the pot — cook in small batches.

steps_template: Light charcoal and wait until coals are covered in white ash (15-20 min).
Clean and oil the grill grates.
Pat ingredients dry and season. Thread onto skewers if needed.
Place on grill over direct heat for searing, indirect heat for slower cooking.
Flip once halfway through. Don't press down on the food.
Baste with sauce in the last few minutes only (sugar burns).
Check internal temperature for doneness. Rest briefly before serving.
tips: Two-zone fire (direct + indirect) gives you the most control.

steps_template: Preheat oven to 425°F / 220°C.
Cut vegetables into uniform pieces (about 1-inch).
Toss with olive oil, salt, and pepper on a baking sheet.
Spread in a single layer — do not crowd.
Roast for 20-30 minutes, flipping halfway, until edges are golden and caramelized.
Finish with fresh herbs, a squeeze of lemon, or a drizzle of balsamic.
tips: High heat and space between pieces = caramelization. Crowding = steaming.

steps_template: Set water bath to the target temperature using the sous vide circulator.
Season protein and place in a vacuum-sealed or zip-lock bag.
Remove air from the bag using water displacement or a vacuum sealer.
Submerge bag in the water bath for the specified time.
Remove from bag and pat dry thoroughly.
Sear in a very hot pan for 30-60 seconds per side for a golden crust.
tips: The sear after sous vide is what gives the Maillard reaction. Don't skip it.

steps_template: Prepare vegetables: wash, cut into desired shapes.
Make brine: dissolve salt in water (2-3 tbsp per quart).
Add flavorings: garlic, ginger, chili, sugar, vinegar as needed.
Pack vegetables into a clean jar. Pour brine over to cover completely.
Seal loosely (fermentation produces gas). Store at room temperature.
Ferment for 3-7 days (taste daily after day 2).
Transfer to refrigerator when desired tanginess is reached.
tips: Keep everything submerged under the brine to prevent mold. Use clean utensils.

steps_template: Place sauce, broth, or liquid in a wide pan over medium-high heat.
Bring to a gentle boil.
Reduce heat to maintain a steady simmer.
Cook until liquid has reduced by half or more, stirring occasionally.
The sauce should coat the back of a spoon.
Season to taste. Strain if desired for a silky texture.
tips: A wider pan = faster reduction. Taste as you go — flavors concentrate.

steps_template: Add ingredients and liquid to the pressure cooker. Do not fill past the max line.
Lock the lid and set the valve to 'sealing'.
Select the appropriate pressure level and time.
When time is up, release pressure naturally (10-15 min) or quick-release.
Open the lid carefully, tilting away from your face.
Stir and adjust seasoning before serving.
tips: Natural release keeps proteins tender. Quick release is better for vegetables.

steps_template: Heat wok over the highest possible flame until it starts to smoke.
Add a small amount of oil — just enough to coat.
Add ingredients in small batches. The wok must stay screaming hot.
Toss ingredients by flicking the wok (not stirring) to expose them to the flame.
The brief contact with open flame creates the signature smoky flavor.
Total cook time per batch: 30-60 seconds.
Remove immediately. Wok hei is lost if food sits.
tips: Home stoves rarely get hot enough. Use the smallest wok you have and cook in tiny batches.

steps_template: Pre-fry or blanch main ingredients (potatoes, cauliflower, meat).
Heat oil in a wok. Fry dried chilies and Sichuan peppercorns until fragrant.
Add aromatics (ginger, garlic, scallions) and stir-fry 30 seconds.
Return main ingredients to wok. Add doubanjiang (chili bean paste).
Stir-fry everything together over high heat for 2-3 minutes.
Season with soy sauce, sugar, and salt. Toss until everything is coated.
Serve in a dry pot with a small burner underneath to keep it sizzling.
tips: The key is minimal sauce — everything should be coated, not swimming.

steps_template: Rinse 1 cup rice thoroughly. Optionally soak for 30 minutes.
Combine rice with 8-10 cups water in a large pot.
Bring to a boil, then reduce to a very low simmer.
Cook for 1.5-2 hours, stirring occasionally to prevent sticking.
The congee is ready when the rice has completely broken down and is creamy.
Season with salt, white pepper, and a drizzle of sesame oil.
Top with scallions, crispy shallots, or other desired toppings.
tips: The ratio of water to rice determines thickness. More water = thinner congee.

steps_template: Prepare filling: cook and season protein and vegetables. Let cool slightly.
Lay out wrapper (rice paper, tortilla, or spring roll skin).
Place filling in the lower third of the wrapper.
Fold the bottom edge over the filling.
Fold in the sides. Roll tightly away from you.
Seal the edge with a dab of water or egg wash.
For spring rolls: deep fry until golden. For fresh rolls: serve immediately.
tips: Don't overfill — it will burst. Keep wrappers moist with a damp towel.

steps_template: Heat oil in a pot. Add curry paste or powder and toast for 1 minute.
Add aromatics (onion, garlic, ginger) and cook until softened.
Add protein and brown on all sides.
Pour in coconut milk, broth, or water. Stir to combine.
Bring to a boil, then simmer for 20-40 minutes.
Add vegetables in the last 10-15 minutes.
Season with fish sauce, lime juice, or sugar to balance flavors.
Serve over rice or with bread.
tips: Toast the curry paste first to bloom the spices. Coconut milk balances heat.

steps_template: Soak wood chips in water for 30 minutes.
Prepare a smoking setup: smoker box or foil packet with holes.
Season protein or fish generously.
Set up indirect heat at 225-275°F / 110-135°C.
Add soaked wood chips to the heat source.
Place food on the grate, close the lid.
Smoke for 1-4 hours depending on the protein, maintaining steady temperature.
The food is done when it reaches the target internal temperature.
tips: Don't peek too often — each opening drops temperature and extends cook time.

steps_template: Fill a pot with 3-4 inches of water. Add a splash of vinegar.
Bring to a gentle simmer (small bubbles, not boiling).
Crack an egg into a small bowl.
Create a gentle whirlpool in the water with a spoon.
Slide the egg into the center of the whirlpool.
Cook for 3-4 minutes for a runny yolk.
Remove with a slotted spoon. Trim any wispy edges. Serve immediately.
tips: Fresh eggs hold together better. The vinegar helps the white set. The whirlpool keeps it compact.

steps_template: Place sugar and a small amount of water in a clean, heavy-bottomed pan.
Heat over medium heat. Do not stir — swirl the pan instead.
Watch the color change: clear → pale gold → amber → deep caramel.
When it reaches the desired color, immediately remove from heat.
Carefully add hot water or other liquid (it will splatter).
Stir to dissolve any hardened caramel. Use immediately.
tips: Caramel goes from perfect to burnt in seconds. Stay attentive. Have everything ready before you start.

steps_template: Bring a large pot of water to a rolling boil. Add salt.
Add noodles and stir immediately to prevent sticking.
Cook according to package directions, stirring occasionally.
For soup noodles: cook to just under al dente (they'll finish in the broth).
For dry noodles: drain when al dente. Rinse with cold water for chewier texture.
Toss with sauce or add to soup. Serve immediately.
tips: Use plenty of water — at least 4 cups per serving. Noodles expand as they cook.

steps_template: Prepare the glaze: combine sweetener (honey, maple, miso) with liquid (soy, vinegar).
Cook protein or vegetables using another method first.
Brush or spoon glaze over the surface in the last 2-3 minutes of cooking.
Let the glaze caramelize under heat (broiler or high stovetop).
Apply a second coat for extra shine and flavor.
Serve immediately — glaze is best when fresh and glossy.
tips: Sugar-based glazes burn easily. Apply at the very end and watch closely.

steps_template: Prepare ingredients: slice thinly and uniformly.
Arrange on dehydrator trays or oven racks in a single layer.
Set temperature to 135-160°F / 57-71°C for fruits/vegetables, 160°F for jerky.
Dehydrate for 6-12 hours, checking periodically.
Food is done when it bends without breaking and has no moisture.
Cool completely before storing in airtight containers.
tips: Slice everything the same thickness for even drying. Flip halfway if using an oven.
